replies.title: Re: Friday the 13th
replies.content:
I can't remember where I first read it, but Friday the 13th being unlucky is relatively modern. Different source-this is from VOX- but this pretty much matches what I remember:

That’s largely thanks to Thomas Lawson’s 1907 book Friday, the Thirteenth. Lawson, a notorious stock market promoter who was essentially a turn-of-the-century Wolf of Wall Street, wrote the book about a fictional stockbroker who chooses that day to deliberately crash the stock market. It’s only a year later that we see the first known mention of Friday the 13th in the media.

So while 13 and Friday have been considered unlucky, putting them together is a modern twist-at least according to a couple of sources.That’s largely thanks to Thomas Lawson’s 1907 book Friday, the Thirteenth. Lawson, a notorious stock market promoter who was essentially a turn-of-the-century Wolf of Wall Street, wrote the book about a fictional stockbroker who chooses that day to deliberately crash the stock market. It’s only a year later that we see the first known mention of Friday the 13th in the media.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Good evening JMG,

1. I noticed that cats seem to be a lot friendlier with me since taking up magical practice. If I cross a random cat on the street, instead of running away, they tend to approach me and lie fat on their backs, something that never happened before. Could there be some truth behind the stereotype of cats being associated to witches and magicians?

2. Are there any occult effects of living in an apartment building rather than some other type of living arrangement, such as a suburban house? Would the energy of all the people surrounding you in an apartment building you tend to interfere with occult practice in any way?

3. Where would you place the individual ego on the Tree of Life? As I understand it, in natal astrology the ego more or less corresponds to the Sun as the individual's basic purpose and identity, but on the Tree of Life, the Tiphareth center is commonly held to belong to the Higher Self, which I'm assuming is trans-egoic. As I see it, a reasonably strong argument against attributing the ego to Tiphareth is that most people have at least semi-functional egos while the vast majority of people have not developed their Tiphareth centers much at all. This would leave Yesod as a natural candidate, but I associate the 9th Sphere more with the subconscious than the conscious mind. I've meditated on this question quite a bit so I'm curious to hear your thoughts.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Most cats love magic. Back when we had one, she would curl up underneath my altar every time I set things up for a ritual. So, yes, quite probably.

2) I've lived in apartment buildings for most of my adult life, and live in one now. It's never slowed me down.

3) The ego is located in one of the four lower spheres depending on where you anchor your identity. If your identity is anchored in your physical appearance it's in Malkuth; if it's anchored in your sexuality, in Yesod; if it's in your lower mind, Hod; and if it's in your emotions, Netzach. Remember that the ego is nothing more than your conception of yourself. It's not actually you, or anything particularly real -- it's just a self-concept. That's why it dissolves once you awaken to your actual identity in Tiphareth.

comments.title: Circles of power
comments.content:
Hi JMG, I recently purchased The Circles of Power (3rd edition). Great book, wish I had it earlier, but there are some things I'm hoping you can clarify.

1. You don't mention the use of signs of the Enterer or Silence for LRP. Will using them make the ritual objectively more powerful? Likewise, the signs of the veil for GRP?

2. In the Vibrational formula for the Middle Pillar, you say vibrate the name of god and send it in the direction of the energy you want to invoke. How do I choose the direction for a sephira on a tree of life? Do I use the GD elemental correspondence for the sephiras?

I have more questions, but I'm trying to meditate on them on my own.

P.S. I can't wait till you release the Order of Spiritual Alchemy course.

replies.title: Re: Circles of power
replies.content:
1) In classic GD practice the signs are not used in those rituals. They're used in more complex ritual work. No, I don't think they'll make those rituals any more powerful.

2) In classic GD practice all the other sephirah are due east of Malkuth.

replies.title: Re: Circles of power
replies.content:
1. Alright, that makes it a lot simpler.

2. Strange, I would've guessed they are up above Malkuth, but it makes it simpler again.

Thank you for answering, Archdruid.

replies.title: Re: Circles of power
replies.content: 2) You can't exactly lie on your back and do the Sign of the Enterer straight up! Since the sephiroth are everywhere, it's purely a convention to assign them the direction of east.

replies.title: Re: Circles of power
replies.content: I don’t know about the rituals being more powerful, but the use of Enterer in the Pentagram rituals has personally proven a useful way to drive home the idea that, in the case of the Enterer, these divine names are informing their respective pentagrams and thus the quarters they occupy; Silence, in turn, serves to give a little comma between each quarter, the use of which may or may not add to your experience as it has mine.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Dear Mr. Greer,

I had two questions related to geomancy. The first is if there are any special precautions or caveats to consider when using geomancy as an aid in making a big life decision?

The second is if there is a guideline as to how frequently geomancy can be used? Could I for example ask multiple questions in a single session?

Thank you for providing this forum.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Practice it until you can get reliable results on less important questions before using it for major questions.

2) As often as you want -- just don't repeat the same question more than once. (People who use that are usually trying to force the oracle to give them the answer they want, which is a bad idea anyway.)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
(Not the OP)

1) Does this mean its advisable to ask about things of less importance - like, should I buy/sell this object - mundane stuff, basically, until we get a feel for it? I tried that and I felt like the oracle got grumpy at me. Otherwise, what sorts of things fit the right category of "practice questions"?

Thank you!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Not necessarily. What I mean is simply that it's probably not a good idea to stake major life decisions with enduring consequences on a reading you do with an oracle you don't really know yet. Meaningful questions are fine.

comments.title: Recipe Thank You, Holiday SOP, Creator Protection Working, Earth Path, Spirit Etheric Bodies
comments.content:
Good evening and a good Lughnasadh to you!

First, not a question, but a more specific thank you. This weekend I went on a camping trip with some friends, and since it was Lughnasadh/Hlaefmaest, I thought it would be a great chance to try out the "Druid Bread" recipe from the Encyclopedia of Natural Magic. It was my first ever time baking bread, and I not only enjoyed making it immensely, everyone agreed it turned out very tasty (though a bit overly dense - I'm not sure if the yeast didn't activate enough or if I over-kneaded, or both, but hey, learning experience). So, thanks very much both for the recipe, and for something you wrote that I read recently about how baking bread is something pretty straightforward that folks have been doing for thousands of years and doesn't require any equipment more sophisticated than a bowl or two, a flat surface, and an oven - it was exactly the nudge I needed, and I found it very rewarding.

1. Related: I'm working through the DMH and so far only have the gate of Air in my SOP practice. By Alban Elued, I'll likely be partway through learning Earth. If I perform the Grove Ceremony for the holy day, should I include the partial SOP, or perform the ritual without the SOP as laid out in the Druid Handbook?

2. I've seen some references to a working you have put on your books to make them not work for those who don't purchase them. I've purchased your books that I have, but I would like to ask about a potential edge case, and if needed, make up for it: I used to travel a lot, and I developed a preference for electronic books that I'm now rethinking. I bought most of these through the usual enormous suspect (something else I'm rethinking). I had a problem with the idea that all of my electronic books could be rendered non-functional by a single corporate decision, so I have removed the DRM from all of my electronic books, allowing me to read them with non-proprietary software. I have not and do not intend to post, share, or otherwise distribute these books to anyone else. Have I harmed you or the publishers by doing so? If so, how might I best make amends?

3. I am currently meditating my way through the elemental correspondence of each few of the Ogham. I'm having a bit of trouble fleshing these correspondences out and not just going over the same basic insights over and over. It feels like the root of the problem is that I don't yet have strong intuitions for what each element is like (that's part of what I'm working to develop, of course!). Are there any sources, meditation themes, or (other) that might help me better get to know the five elements?

4. I'm pairing the DH with the DMH, and one of the sections I'm (predictably) struggling with is the Earth Path. I'm noticing ways that I'm being wasteful, but having trouble identifying alternatives. One example: I burn candles as part of daily prayer. They're colored to suit the deity honored by each candle. That's a lot of paraffin! I've read that beeswax candles are healthier, and I'd like to try buying some beeswax from local beekeepers to make some of my own, but my experience of buying commercial beeswax from larger suppliers is that this might be a bit expensive for ongoing religious/magical use. Any thoughts on striking the right balance?

5. Do spirits of the astral or higher planes have etheric bodies? My current understanding is "no" - instead, they can affect nwfre in some ways, just as they can affect the physical world in some ways, but are not actually manifest in etheric/material vehicles. (Not asking for any practical working, just curious and trying to develop my understanding of the planes and subtle bodies from different angles).

replies.title: Re: Recipe Thank You, Holiday SOP, Creator Protection Working, Earth Path, Spirit Etheric Bodies
replies.content:
0) Glad to hear it. Very often when it comes out too dense, you're pushing too hard when you knead -- concentrate on gently stretching the dough to build the gluten.

1) Until you have the whole SoP together, leave it out of seasonal rituals.

2) You're fine. I didn't authorize the DRM and have no stake in it remaining in place.

3) The best way I know to enrich your sense of the elements is to deal with their physical forms. Spend more time paying attention to fires, winds, water, and earth, and to the presence of life in them,

4) Only you can find that balance. I don't know what your budget can handle!

5) It depends very much on the type of spirit! There are immense numbers of different kinds; some have etheric bodies, most do not.

replies.title: Re: Physical Effects from Magic
replies.content:
Invisibility (of sorts) is an interesting magical practice. Here's a simple exercise anyone can do that will shed some light on how it very often works.

Go into one of those busy sandwich chains at noon, when the line is very long, and stand in line for a sandwich. As the line inches forward, empty yourself of any desire for a sandwich, any sense of being hungry, any impatience for a meal. Very often you will find that the server serves the person in front of you, and then gets ready to serve the person behind you, leaving you unserved between them. At that moment let your strong desire for a sandwich come roaring back. Often as not, the server will do a double-take, notice you for the first time, and react to your presence--usually with something like, "Sorry, for a moment I didn't see you there."

The server isn't noticing people as people at all, but is simply reacting to each successive set of subtle cues that he now has to deal with yet another impatient case of an unsatisfied appetite. No appetite = no subtle cues = no awareness that you/b> are there in the endless line of unsatisfied appetites.

You can extend this method to all sorts of other situations, once you figure out what sort of subtle cues the other people are expecting as they interact with the crowd. A fair amount of magic doesn't involve anything supernatural at all, just a really deep understanding of how other people go through life on autopilot, and the skill at using that understanding to one's own advantage. Part of this is what the stage magician calls the art of "misdirecting attention"; it's a very useful art that occultists should (IMHO) also master.

In a Darwinian sense, all humanity needed to do to achieve its current multi-billions of breeding individuals is to become "just barely intelligent enough and perceptive enough" to gain an advantage in biological evolution over other species. Any more intelligence and perceptiveness than that would hardly serve the interests of our DNA. (One might almost think that all the various species and the individuals--you and me--in them are nothing more than robotic slaves created by our DNA to serve its own interests. But even slaves can sometimes escape their masters, and more often they find effective ways to thwart them.) So we humans actually are not a very intelligent species at all, and this stupidity of ours has given us a Darwinian advantage in the past.

You're on the brink of realizing the key to the Tetragrammaton -- it's a scheme that you can and should apply to any given narrative in many different ways -- and also of realizing that different accounts of creation are not necessarily convertible into each other. The reality of creation is not something that human minds at their present stage of evolution is able to handle, at all. What we have are symbolic narratives, each of which teaches us something about the process -- and those narratives have about as much to do with one another as the reports of the famous blind men who had hold of different parts of the elephant.

These things are tools, not truths, and they do not say the same thing in the same way. It's when you can hold them in consciousness that way, as metaphors that teach you something about the universe of your experience, that they begin to do their real work.

I suppose this might be totally seperate to the magic, but it just seems a bit uncanny and almost like a cruel twist to what I've asked for.

The firing also seperated me from the bloke I'd fallen for...

replies.title: Re: Love Magic going wrong
replies.content:
This sort of thing is very common. Nowadays there are a lot of magical workings available online and in books, and people think they can just pick one up and it'll work for them. Quite often it doesn't -- as you've found out!

What happened with the love spell was typical magical blowback -- there wasn't anyone available for the spell to land on, so it boomeranged back onto the caster. What happened with the protection spell is another kind of spellwork gone awry: you got what you wanted -- you no longer have to worry about the woman at your former job -- but the way the spell got it for you wasn't exactly what you had in mind. Again, this is typical.

I can only pass on the good advice that's been handed down in occult schools for a very long time: if you want to practice magic successfully you have to go through a proper course of training. Trying to do magic for yourself without that background is rather like trying to do surgery on yourself without going to medical school first. The good news is that you clearly have enough talent for magic to get effects -- even if they weren't the effects you wanted. The question is whether you want to go further, and learn how it's done.

comments.title: The Piscean Christ and the Aquarian Christ
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

I wanted to share my perspective from living in a Catholic country that doesn't seem to be losing its marbles over every little foible coming out of the bureaucratic Church these days.

In the years I've lived here, several issues have come from the Vatican that local church leaders made no comment on, as if nothing had happened, or if they did issue a statement, it was along the lines of "We're going to keep doing what we were doing; it seems to work for us."

There is a co-existing diversity here that seems to work. It's still too early to see how things will shake out with the Pope restricting the Latin Mass, but I wouldn't be surprised if it gets the same "meh" response locally, and parishes keep doing whatever they were doing before.

It is thus my hope that this local flavor of Catholicism will continue onward in its co-existing diversity, as both polarized extremes in Rome spin out into absurdity and meet the Ring-Pass-Not. This will make way for the Aquarian Christians of the future, I hope.

Catholicism is already very earthy here. One of my first impressions was seeing the altars to guardian saints at the front of crop fields. It reminded me of when I lived in Japan and would come across small Shinto altars periodically on hikes in the forest.

replies.title: Re: The Piscean Christ and the Aquarian Christ
replies.content: For what it is worth, most of the Catholics in my Midwestern diocese are neither the hardcore traditionalists nor the wishy-washy modernist liberals that seem so prominent in Church leadership now.  The best advice I got on practicing the Catholic religion came from a former SSPX seminarian: most Catholics throughout history knew very little about whoever happened to be pope and knew very little of the squabbles and fights in the Church's politics.  Instead, they prayed for the pope, had their devotions, received the sacraments from their parish priest, and lived their lives.

replies.title: Re: The Piscean Christ and the Aquarian Christ
replies.content: I've been told that this is normally the case in a country where Catholicism has deep roots. Part of what makes the Catholic church in the US so unsteady is that it doesn't have those roots; I'm not at all sure it will ever get them.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
FWIW, we recently had to enter the rental market, and found the online application process so error-ridden and Byzantine that it was impossible for us to even *apply* to rent a house or apartment-- on some of the applications one had to make factually contradictory statements in order to move on to the next page, such as "Do you own a dog? (no)" followed by a *required* field asking if your dog is up to date on its shots (yes? no? Uhhh?), and whether your dog is over or under forty pounds (Gah! I haven't got a dog!). It's not just government agencies!

I think everything that has gone "conveniently" online but requires a detailed application... has gotten increasingly buggy and difficult to navigate in recent years. It helps to remember that these systems aren't designed to be helpful: they're designed so that the entity on the other end can winnow out all but the most doggedly determined applicants while making as little actual human contact as possible: online access opened the process up to the whole world, but nobody has the staff to deal with the whole world!

The solution, as far as I can tell, is to show up in person. Say you don't have internet, or more truthfully, that you were having trouble navigating the online application process and you'd like a paper form to fill out, and someone to answer questions you have about it, so that you can do it properly. Now you're not an anonymous partially-filled form going automatically into the waste bin. You're a real, live, breathing human being, annoyingly sitting in their office, that they have to interact with.

FWIW, I see this as a hopeful development. Applying for anything that matters, online, is becoming increasingly frustrating and impossible... and is forcing a return to a more sane and humane way of doing business. It's the devil if you're trying to get a rental house or a job somewhere away from your current home, but I think ultimately, it'll lead to a revival of local economies.

comments.title: Shamanic journey-ing
comments.content:
Me again.

I started spontaneously doing these meditation/visualisations a few years back where I would go down some steps, into a room with 4 doors. I would enter the second door and be on a flat grassy plain with a tree in the middle. I would go up to the tree and see people I knew in my life, stood still, awake but not interactive. I would talk to the people and connect and resolve things that were happening in waking life.

I found that some of the work I have done in that place has been effective in a more subtle and desirable way than the 'magic magic' I've attempted.

Also I get insights and see entities in the place, some of them not pleasant, others good.

Do you have any thoughts about what this place is and do you know whether this is a recognisable form of magical practice?

I found out later about shamanic journeying, but I don't feel like this 100% fits with that.

replies.title: Re: Shamanic journey-ing
replies.content: This is a very standard practice in occult schools. Your imagination is the interface between you and the astral plane, and developing the imagination by way of inner places and journeys like this is a good way to develop things. One thing to keep in mind, however, is that "resolving things" with your images of people could amount to doing magic on them without their consent -- and that's a very risky thing to do.

replies.title: Re: Shamanic journey-ing

replies.title: Re: Shamanic journey-ing
replies.content: The most powerful magic is always work on yourself.

replies.title: Re: Shamanic journey-ing
replies.content:
Find what deep seated issue or 'part' of yourself contributes to the unhealthy patterns with these people then give it a form and work on transforming it on the journeys.

At least that's how I would approach it if my visualization were better.

replies.title: Re: Shamanic journey-ing

I have learned how to do quite competent divination, but otherwise lack real magical skills. However, lately I've been "seeing" things like tanks rolling down the streets, or buildings that have been damaged or knocked over, or a shopping mall turned into a refugee camp. I've done divinations, and determined that these are premonitions of a civil war here in Canada. I live in Ottawa, which in any kind of civil war is going to be hit rather hard: given the status as national capital, it will be a major centre of fighting no matter what the cause of the conflict.

I don't expect it to happen over night, but judging from my divination, it will be here within 5 years at the latest. I am currently very, very poor and lack the money to move, even just to a different city (and that's if I could get a job there). Even so, I've severely tightened my budget to try to get some savings, started planning for how I'd handle major disruptions to things like food, water, or electricity, and am looking at my options for getting into a skilled or semi-skilled trade. At the mundane level, I'd say I am doing everything I can think of, which is why I'm now looking into the occult.

In terms of the occult, I'm doing divinations to try to figure out my options, and have started working through the Celtic Golden Dawn with the idea of being able to learn to bring more energies into improving my situation. Are there any forms of magic I can use or learn fairly quickly at this point in time beyond this to increase my chances of either getting somewhere safer in the next few years or being able to weather the coming storm with as much grace as possible if I end up unable to get out?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Okay, first of all be aware that not all such premonitions come to pass. It's wise to prepare for trouble but don't assume that your visions are necessarily correct -- a lot of people who assume their visions are true get blindsided when things don't go the way they thought. (Did you know anybody who insisted that the world was going to end in 2012? I did.)

Second, no, the best thing to do is put your efforts into working on one system of magic, and do it as systematically and intensively as you can. The CGD is as good as any, and if you work it hard, you should be able to do serious magical work within a year or two.

However, I'm not particularly thrilled about Ottawa's long term viability anyway, so getting out still seems like a good idea. Thinking ahead, Ottawa seems like it won't be nearly as pleasant a place to live in a decade or two even if we avoid any kind of major crisis: there's too many people, and the economy is too dependent on the government.

In my experience divination, but I've only done it seriously for about two years, also opens you up to sensing what is being thrown out to the emotional and mental commons and its hard to distinguish between the two. comments.title: Thoughts on materialism, technology, Steiner's Ahriman
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

as always, thanks for contributing to the management of this public good.

I have been listening to Rudolf Steiner's lectures on the the Influences of Lucifer and Ahriman for the earth in the past weeks, as I found some interesting material that i could freely access as an audio, and I get to do this whilst doing some repetitive work in the PC (Building databases can be quite monotonous).

I am not sure if you are familiar with Steiner's thesis of Ahriman (as a separate entity), but as a concept it is basically one of the "poles" of evil, an entity that itself thrives in materialism devoid of spirituality, over-intellectualism. The other pole is luceferian, characterised by egotism, inflation, passionality. Anyway, as I have heard these lectures, the description of the ailments that Steiner saw in the early 20th century.. such as an unabashed reliance on technology and how it displaces humanity in many fields, seemed extremely accurate. Automatization, big data, virtual reality, drones coded to kill, nuclear weapons, all of it seemed very much explained by this influence that Steiner mentions.. is bound to "incarnate" in the future. It was very prescient indeed.

Now, putting the prophesies aside, it does seem to me that this drive towards materialism, which inherently also fervently denies the existence of different (AKA spiritual) models of reality is driving us more and more to the description of Steiner's Ahriman. A very interesting blogger, Dimitry Orlov, which I follow due to his contributions to collapse literature, actually describes something eerily similar.. altho as the "technosphere", but with remarkable similarities to Steiner's Ahriman (altho Orlov does not approach the subject from a spiritual perspective at all).

I took some time to search your views on Steiner, which I found in a 2017 open post at ecosophia. I understood that you do have some knowledge on his work, and wanted to know if you have any specific thoughts as to the issue I just raised.

replies.title: Re: Thoughts on materialism, technology, Steiner's Ahriman
replies.content: Modern industrial society is very Ahrimanic, though there's a corresponding Luciferic imbalance, which you can see in the corrupt end of the New Age movement and the like. I think Steiner's wrong that either pole will "incarnate" in any sense  other than the one you see around you right now, though.

replies.title: Re: Thoughts on materialism, technology, Steiner's Ahriman
replies.content: Do you know anything which discusses the imbalance in modern industrial society as it relates to Ahrimanic evil in more depth?

replies.title: Re: Thoughts on materialism, technology, Steiner's Ahriman
replies.content: No, I've just read Steiner on the subject and found his concepts useful in this context.

replies.title: Re: Thoughts on materialism, technology, Steiner's Ahriman
replies.content: Thank you for the response. Is it right to associate this two "extremes" with forces in the pillars of qabalah? And Christ being the middle pillar represented by the middle pillar (and the reason why its so valuable to do a MP as a ritual exceraise?). If thats not the case (or of it is) any other ritual advice to be less influenced by Aheamanic and Luciferic influences ?

replies.title: Re: Thoughts on materialism, technology, Steiner's Ahriman
replies.content: Yes -- Ahriman is the unbalanced pillar of force and Lucifer the unbalanced pillar of form, with Christ (or, on a lower plane, the archangel Michael) holding the balance between them. Any ritual -- and there are plenty of them -- that works with the point of balance between opposites will help with this.

comments.title: Voodoo Dolls
comments.content:
I've recently heard of a trend of (mostly white) people making "Voodoo Dolls", basically fetish objects in the shape of a person, sometimes with hair/nails/blood of the person in question, sometimes not, and not always for malefic intent, either- the idea of "White pins" being useful for healing, and such things.

My question is: What sort of blowback does this sort of thing have? Aside from the karma of someone using magic on someone else without their permission(though thankfully many people do emphasize the importance of consent), my understanding is that this isn't really being done as part of an initiatory practice of Voudou, Vodun, or any of the other diasporic traditions where the term "Voodoo Doll" comes from. Would that lead to much more severe blowback, if they don't know what they're doing, or how it's supposed to fit with the rest of the tradition? Or perhaps less blowback since they don't understand how to do it in the first place?

That being so, are there other traditions where similar fetish objects are created? Would those have less severe consequences, even if people still called them "Voodoo Dolls"?

replies.title: Re: Voodoo Dolls
replies.content: Many traditions of folk magic make poppets -- that's the trad English word for them. If it's being done without consent and/or with negative intentions, yes, there'll be plenty of blowback to go around.
